sections.brief_summary: Introduction to Imamate, outlining Sunni-Shia debate on obligation of appointing a leader.
Rational argument against divine appointment of Imam due to harms like fitna and concealment.
Critique of Shia responses on divine support and occultation as contrary to reason.
Refutation of occultation necessity, comparing to prophets who did not hide.
Arguments against prolonged occultation of the Awaited Imam as irrational.
Historical critique of Shia claims on Imam's concealment and lack of pursuit.

sections.content_arabic: وَ كَأَيِّنْ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ قَاتَلَ مَعَهُ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ...
sections.brief_summary: Quranic proof that prophets faced enemies without hiding, refuting fear-based occultation.

sections.content_arabic: عن أبي حمزة عن علي بن الحسين...
sections.brief_summary: Hadith distinguishing grief from fear, refuting claims against Imams' bravery.
Conclusion on irrationality of divine appointment and occultation.
Second belief: Imam must be manifest; Quranic proofs for visible leadership.
Quranic verses requiring Imam's power for jihad, prayer, and order.

sections.content_arabic: إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ بَعَثَ لَكُمْ طَالُوتَ مَلِكاً...
sections.brief_summary: Third belief: Infallibility not required; examples of non-infallible leaders like Talut.
Refutation of Shia arguments for infallibility via rational proofs.
Fourth belief: No divine nass needed; appointment by people.
Fifth belief: Imam need not be most superior.
Sixth belief: Abu Bakr immediate successor; defense of caliphs.
Quranic proof for caliphate of first three via succession promise.
Hadith proofs from Nahj al-Balagha affirming caliphs.
Refutation of Shia proofs like Ayat al-Wilaya.
Summary of Shia sects' disagreements on Imams.
